title: Deals
lede: Deals turn conversations into revenue you can see coming. Track every opportunity through your pipeline — value, probability, stage, and the people behind it — then convert winners into quotes, invoices, and jobs without ever re-typing a thing.

steps.title: Open the Deals list
steps.content:
Your pipeline lives here: every open opportunity, its dollar value, and how likely it is to close — all on one screen.

  1. In the top navigation, click CRM, then click the Deals tab (or go directly to /pipeline/deals).
  2. Every deal appears as a row with these columns: Deal Name, Value, Probability (color-coded: green = hot, red = long shot), Stage (a colored pill like Proposal or Negotiation), Account, Contacts, Leads, Expected Close, Source, Tags, Status (Open / Won / Lost), Owner, and Actions.
  3. The table scrolls sideways for the extra columns; the total (Total N deals) shows under the table with page controls.
  4. Click a deal's name (or the green eye icon in Actions) to open its full detail page. Clicking an Account or a Contact in a row jumps straight to that record.

A deal is one potential sale — "Rooftop solar for Brightline HQ, $12,500, 50% likely to close this month." Leads and contacts are the people; the deal is the money conversation you're having with them.

images.alt: Deals list showing deal rows with value, probability, stage pills, account and contacts columns
images.caption: The Deals list — your whole pipeline with values, probabilities, and stage pills at a glance

images.alt: Deals list showing deal rows with value, probability, stage pills, account and contacts columns
images.caption: The Deals list — your whole pipeline with values, probabilities, and stage pills at a glance

steps.title: Create a deal
steps.content:
1. Click Create Deal in the top-right of the list. The Create New Deal form opens.
  2. Enter a Deal Name (required) — make it self-explanatory, like "Rooftop Solar Installation — Brightline HQ".
  3. Optionally add a Description with the scope or context.
  4. Pick the Pipeline and the Stage (both required). Choosing a stage auto-sets the Probability (%) to that stage's default — you can still drag the slider or type an exact percentage.
  5. Enter the Deal Value and pick the currency (USD, EUR, GBP, and more) from the dropdown on its left.
  6. Set the Expected Close Date — this powers your forecast and the Expected Close column.
  7. Link the people: Associated Account (the company), Associated Contacts, and Associated Leads (both multi-select). The + button next to each picker creates a brand-new account, contact, or lead on the spot without leaving the form.
  8. Optionally pick a Source (Website, Referral, Cold Call, Email Campaign, Social Media, Event, Other) and add Tags (type and press Enter).
  9. Click Create Deal in the top-right.
⚠️ Every deal must be linked to at least one Account, Contact, or Lead — a deal with nobody behind it can't be saved. If saving fails, link a person or company and try again.

images.alt: Create New Deal form filled with name, description, pipeline, stage, value, close date, account, contact, source and tags
images.caption: The Create New Deal form — stage picks set the probability automatically, and + buttons quick-create accounts, contacts, or leads

images.alt: Create New Deal form filled with name, description, pipeline, stage, value, close date, account, contact, source and tags
images.caption: The Create New Deal form — stage picks set the probability automatically, and + buttons quick-create accounts, contacts, or leads

steps.title: Search, filter, and sort the pipeline
steps.content:
Slice the pipeline any way you need:

  1. Click Show Filters (top-right, next to Create Deal). A filter row appears:
  • Search deals... — type part of a deal name and press Enter.
  • Filter by pipeline — pick a pipeline; this unlocks Filter by stage to narrow to one stage of that pipeline.
  • Start date → End date — limit to deals created in a date range.
  • Filter by owner — see one teammate's deals only.

Two more tricks live in the table itself:

  • Sorting — click the Value, Probability, or Expected Close header to sort the whole pipeline (not just the current page); click again to flip direction.
  • Status filter — the small filter icon on the Status column header lets you show only Open, Won, or Lost deals.
💡 Sort by Value descending with the Status filter on Open — instant "biggest deals on the table" view for your Monday pipeline review.

images.alt: Filter row with search box, pipeline filter, stage filter, date range and owner filter
images.caption: The filter row — search, pipeline + stage, date range, and owner

images.alt: Filter row with search box, pipeline filter, stage filter, date range and owner filter
images.caption: The filter row — search, pipeline + stage, date range, and owner

steps.title: Open a deal — the Overview tab
steps.content:
Click any deal to open its detail page. The Overview tab starts with four stat tiles:

  • Deal Value — the full amount if you win.
  • Weighted Value — value × probability. A $12,500 deal at 50% counts as $6,250 of realistic pipeline — add these up across deals and you have an honest forecast.
  • Probability — color-coded from red (≤25%) to green (≥75%).
  • Current Stage — where the deal sits in the pipeline.

Below the tiles sit the Related Entities card (next step) and the full details table: name, description, value, probability, pipeline, stage, Account / Contacts / Leads (all clickable — contacts and leads open in a side drawer), Source, Expected Close and Actual Close, Status, Loss Reason, Tags, and created/updated dates.

One field deserves a callout: Primary Recipient. When you send a quote or invoice created from this deal, this dropdown decides who it's addressed to — the linked account, or a specific contact or lead. Change it any time.

images.alt: Deal detail page with stat tiles for deal value, weighted value, probability and current stage
images.caption: The deal detail page — stat tiles up top, Related Entities and the details table below

images.alt: Deal detail page with stat tiles for deal value, weighted value, probability and current stage
images.caption: The deal detail page — stat tiles up top, Related Entities and the details table below

images.alt: Deal details table with pipeline, stage, account, contacts, leads, primary recipient, source, dates, status and tags
images.caption: The details table — every field in one place, including the Primary Recipient picker for quotes and invoices

images.alt: Deal details table with pipeline, stage, account, contacts, leads, primary recipient, source, dates, status and tags
images.caption: The details table — every field in one place, including the Primary Recipient picker for quotes and invoices

steps.title: Move the stage and edit the deal
steps.content:
The header of every open deal has your day-to-day controls:

  1. Stage dropdown — pick a new stage (for example Proposal → Negotiation) and the deal moves instantly. Every move is recorded in the Activities timeline.
  2. Edit — flips the whole Overview into edit mode: name, description, value, probability, pipeline, stage, account, contacts, leads, source, expected close date, and tags all become editable in place. Click Save to apply or Cancel to discard.
💡 Changing the Pipeline while editing resets the stage to the new pipeline's first stage — double-check the stage before saving.

images.alt: Deal header controls with stage dropdown, Mark as Won, Mark as Lost and Edit buttons
images.caption: The header controls — move the stage, Mark as Won / Mark as Lost, and Edit

images.alt: Deal header controls with stage dropdown, Mark as Won, Mark as Lost and Edit buttons
images.caption: The header controls — move the stage, Mark as Won / Mark as Lost, and Edit

steps.title: Create quotes, invoices, and jobs from the deal
steps.content:
This is where the pipeline meets paperwork — and where iMash saves you from re-typing. The Related Entities card on the Overview tab shows counters for Quotes, Invoices, Jobs, and their combined Total Value.

  1. Click Create Quote, Create Invoice, or Create Job in the card's top-right.
  2. The full builder opens in a window, already linked to this deal — build the document and save. See Quotes and Invoices for the builders themselves.
  3. Each linked record appears as a row with its Number, Title, Status, Amount, and Date. The eye icon under Actions opens it for viewing or editing right from the deal.

The link works in both directions, automatically: create a quote or invoice anywhere else in iMash and the platform creates (or updates) a matching deal for it — you'll spot these as [Auto] deals in the list. Your pipeline reflects reality even when your team skips a step.

images.alt: Related Entities card with counters for quotes, invoices, jobs and total value plus Create Quote, Create Invoice and Create Job buttons
images.caption: Related Entities on a fresh deal — one click creates a quote, invoice, or job that's linked automatically

images.alt: Related Entities card with counters for quotes, invoices, jobs and total value plus Create Quote, Create Invoice and Create Job buttons
images.caption: Related Entities on a fresh deal — one click creates a quote, invoice, or job that's linked automatically

images.alt: Related Entities card showing a linked quote row with number, title, status, amount and date
images.caption: A linked quote on the deal — number, status, amount, and a one-click view/edit action

images.caption: A linked quote on the deal — number, status, amount, and a one-click view/edit action

steps.title: Activities, Notes, and Files
steps.content:
Three more tabs keep the deal's whole story in one place:

  • Activities — an automatic timeline: Deal Created, every Stage Changed move, edits, and win/loss events, each timestamped. Nobody has to "log" anything — it just happens.
  • Notes — type in Add a note... and click Add Note (or press Enter). Notes show the author and time; the ⋮ menu on each note lets you Edit or Delete it. Perfect for "asked for battery-backup pricing — following up Thursday".
  • Files — attach proposals, site photos, or signed documents directly to the deal.

images.alt: Activities timeline showing Deal Created and Stage Changed entries with timestamps
images.caption: Activities — every stage move and edit is logged automatically

images.alt: Activities timeline showing Deal Created and Stage Changed entries with timestamps
images.caption: Activities — every stage move and edit is logged automatically

images.alt: Notes tab with Add a note input and two timestamped notes with author names
images.caption: Notes — a running journal on the deal, with edit and delete on every entry

images.alt: Notes tab with Add a note input and two timestamped notes with author names
images.caption: Notes — a running journal on the deal, with edit and delete on every entry

steps.title: Close the deal — Won or Lost
steps.content:
Every deal ends one of two ways, and both are one click from the header:

  1. Mark as Won 🏆 — a confirmation window shows the deal value; click Confirm Win. The deal gets a green Won badge, its Actual Close date is stamped, and it's counted in your won revenue.
  2. Mark as Lost — pick a Loss Reason (required: Price Too High, Lost to Competitor, Bad Timing, No Budget, No Decision Made, or Other), optionally add Additional Details, then click Confirm Loss. The reason is saved on the deal — gold for spotting patterns later.

Closed deals are read-only: the stage dropdown and Won/Lost buttons disappear and Edit is disabled, so your history stays clean. Filter the list's Status column by Won or Lost to review closed business any time.

⚠️ Closing a deal can't be un-clicked from the header, so confirm the right deal is open before you hit Confirm Win or Confirm Loss.

images.alt: Mark Deal as Won confirmation window showing the deal value and Confirm Win button
images.caption: Mark as Won — one confirmation and the revenue is booked

images.alt: Mark Deal as Won confirmation window showing the deal value and Confirm Win button
images.caption: Mark as Won — one confirmation and the revenue is booked

images.alt: Mark Deal as Lost window with required Loss Reason dropdown and Additional Details text area
images.caption: Mark as Lost — the required loss reason becomes your competitive intelligence

images.alt: Mark Deal as Lost window with required Loss Reason dropdown and Additional Details text area
images.caption: Mark as Lost — the required loss reason becomes your competitive intelligence

images.alt: Closed deal header showing the Won badge and disabled Edit button
images.caption: A won deal — green badge, locked for editing, history preserved

images.alt: Closed deal header showing the Won badge and disabled Edit button
images.caption: A won deal — green badge, locked for editing, history preserved

steps.title: Make the pipeline yours
steps.content:
The stages you saw (Prospecting → Qualification → Proposal → Negotiation → Closed Won / Closed Lost) are just the default Sales Pipeline. In CRM Settings you can rename stages, change their colors and default probabilities, and even run multiple pipelines side by side (say, one for new business and one for renewals) — the Deals list and every deal form pick the changes up instantly.

Set your stages once, keep probabilities honest, and the Weighted Value tiles across your deals become a forecast you can actually plan a business around. That's the whole game.

support.content:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io

metaDescription: Complete guide to deals in iMash: create deals, move them through pipeline stages, forecast with weighted values, link quotes, invoices and jobs, and close deals as won or lost.
